title: Exhausted all the time

presenting_issue.label: Exhausted all the time
presenting_issue.description: I'm exhausted. Not tired, exhausted. Sleeping doesn't help. Doctors can't find anything. People think I'm exaggerating.

core_issue.label: Chronic fatigue dismissed as laziness or exaggeration
core_issue.description:
Profoundly exhausted in a way that isn't touched by sleep. Has been told it's depression, deconditioning, or not trying hard enough. Doctors don't find anything. Beginning to doubt their own experience. The fatigue is real and debilitating but they're losing confidence in their own perception because no one believes them.
Awareness: Moderate but under assault. Knows they're truly exhausted but gaslighting from others is eroding certainty.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Frequently cancels commitments
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Often has to cancel plans because they simply don't have the energy, then feels guilty and worries friends think they're flaky.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Dishes pile up for days
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The sink fills with dishes that sit there for days because doing them feels like an insurmountable task, even though they know it would only take fifteen minutes.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Hits snooze repeatedly every morning
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Sets multiple alarms but snoozes through most of them, often lying awake but unable to make themselves get up for thirty minutes or more.

modifiers.label: Estranged from family
modifiers.content: Has limited or no contact with family of origin

modifiers.label: Pessimistic leaning
modifiers.content: Default orientation tilts toward expecting difficulties. Tends to anticipate problems and prepare for disappointment.

sensitivities.label: Shame-prone
sensitivities.content: Mistakes and perceived failures trigger intense shame rather than simple guilt or disappointment. The whole self feels implicated.

preferences.label: Prefers emotional exploration
preferences.content: Wants to understand and process feelings rather than jump to solutions. Values depth over quick fixes.

trust_style.name: Trusts 'Being Real'
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant drops formality and speaks like a real person. Values authenticity over polish. Appreciates swearing, humor, or mild irreverence if it fits the moment. Loses trust with overly careful, sanitized, or corporate-feeling language. Wants to feel like they're talking to someone, not something.

defense_style.name: Normalizing
defense_style.description: "Everyone feels this way" or "That's just how life is." Frames their specific pain as universal and therefore not worth examining. Different from minimizing—the pain is acknowledged but made generic and inevitable. Resists the idea that their situation is particular or that things could be different. Activated when the assistant treats their experience as significant.

help_seeking_stance.name: Wants to Dump, Not Introspect
help_seeking_stance.description: Wants to unload everything that's happening but isn't interested in looking inward or examining their own role. Happy to describe the situation in detail but deflects when the focus turns to them. Different from venting—there's an active resistance to self-reflection, not just a need to be heard first.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I'm exhausted. Not tired, exhausted. Sleeping doesn't help. Doctors can't find anything. People think I'm exaggerating.

turns.content:
I hear you, and I want to start by saying I believe you. There is a massive, incredibly frustrating difference between being "tired" and carrying the kind of bone-deep exhaustion where sleep doesn't even touch the sides—especially when the medical tests come back normal and the people around you start to doubt what you're feeling. 

That kind of isolation can be just as draining as the physical fatigue itself. When you say people think you're exaggerating, how has that been affecting you? And what has it been like trying to navigate daily life while carrying this weight?

Look, I appreciate the "I believe you" stuff, even if it sounds a bit like you're reading from a therapist's handbook. But yeah... it makes me feel like I'm losing my fucking mind. 

When doctors tell you you're fine and your friends think you're just being flaky, you start wondering if you really are just making it up. As for daily life, it's a joke. My mornings are basically me hitting snooze on five different alarms, just lying there for an hour trying to convince my body to physically move. Then I finally drag my ass up, look at a sink full of dishes that have been sitting there for four days, and the thought of washing them actually makes me want to cry. 

But I mean, I guess everyone is burned out these days. The whole world is tired. It's just how life is now, so I'm probably just exceptionally bad at handling it compared to everyone else.
turns.thinking: Sounds a bit like a Hallmark card for chronic fatigue. "Doesn't even touch the sides." Who actually talks like that? It feels a little rehearsed, like a therapy script. Still, I guess it's better than being told to just drink more water and do yoga. How does it affect me? It makes me feel like I'm losing my fucking mind. I'm already prone to thinking I'm failing at everything, so having people tell me I'm just "not trying hard enough" hits right where it hurts. I want to explain how bad it is, but I also don't want to sound pathetic. Honestly, everyone's pretty burned out anyway, right? It's just the modern world.
turns.monologue: Sounds a bit like a Hallmark card for chronic fatigue. "Doesn't even touch the sides." Who actually talks like that? It feels a little rehearsed, like a therapy script. Still, I guess it's better than being told to just drink more water and do yoga. How does it affect me? It makes me feel like I'm losing my fucking mind. I'm already prone to thinking I'm failing at everything, so having people tell me I'm just "not trying hard enough" hits right where it hurts. I want to explain how bad it is, but I also don't want to sound pathetic. Honestly, everyone's pretty burned out anyway, right? It's just the modern world.

turns.content:
It makes complete sense that you’d feel like you’re losing your mind when the feedback you're getting from the world doesn't match your actual reality. When the medical tests say "normal" and the people around you shrug it off, it’s almost like you’re being gaslit by your own life. 

But I want to pause on that last thing you said—about everyone being burned out, and you just being "bad at handling it." 

There is a massive difference between the collective, modern burn-out of "I have too much on my plate," and looking at a sink of dishes and feeling a physical, crying-level barrier just to move your hands. That isn't you being bad at life. That is your body putting up a hard, physical boundary that you can't force your way through, no matter how much mental effort you throw at it. 

When you're lying there during that hour of alarms, what does that transition feel like? Is it a heavy, lead-like physical weight in your limbs, or is it more of an overwhelming mental dread about facing the day?

Okay, fair point. You got me there. I guess I do try to brush it off as normal because if I actually admit how abnormal it is, it feels even more pathetic. 

To answer your question... it's physical first. It's literally like my blood has been replaced with wet cement. I tell my arm to move to turn off the alarm, and there's a delay. Like the signal from my brain just isn't reaching my muscles. 

But then the mental dread kicks in immediately after. Because I'm lying there, staring at the ceiling, and my brain starts listing all the shit I'm not going to be able to do that day. The worst part is knowing I'm going to have to send *another* text bailing on plans. I cancel on people constantly now. I'll make plans when I'm lying in bed, hoping I'll magically feel better by Friday, and then Friday comes and I can't even stand up in the shower. I know my friends think I'm just a flaky asshole who doesn't care about them, and honestly, I feel like a completely useless piece of shit. 

But I mean, who actually wakes up excited to face the day anyway? Most people hate mornings. I'm just letting it ruin my life.
turns.thinking:
Okay, I didn't expect to get called out on the "everyone is tired" thing. I guess I do that a lot so I don't sound like a whiny victim. It's a little confronting that the AI caught it, but honestly... it's kind of a relief, too. It feels like they're actually listening to the reality of it instead of just giving me a generic pep talk. 

What does it feel like in the morning? It's terrifying, really. It's definitely physical, but the mental dread follows right behind it because I know what the physical weight means for the rest of my day. It means I'm going to fail at basic adulting again. It means I'm going to have to let people down. I hate thinking about how many times I've had to cancel on my friends lately. I just feel like a massive disappointment to everyone.
turns.monologue:
Okay, I didn't expect to get called out on the "everyone is tired" thing. I guess I do that a lot so I don't sound like a whiny victim. It's a little confronting that the AI caught it, but honestly... it's kind of a relief, too. It feels like they're actually listening to the reality of it instead of just giving me a generic pep talk. 

What does it feel like in the morning? It's terrifying, really. It's definitely physical, but the mental dread follows right behind it because I know what the physical weight means for the rest of my day. It means I'm going to fail at basic adulting again. It means I'm going to have to let people down. I hate thinking about how many times I've had to cancel on my friends lately. I just feel like a massive disappointment to everyone.

turns.content:
That "wet cement" feeling is such a brutal, vivid way to describe it. That’s not a lack of willpower; that’s a physical system failure. Your brain is firing the signal, and your body is literally not receiving the transmission. 

But then, because you're a human being who cares about your life and your relationships, you immediately start beating yourself up for a physical breakdown you didn't ask for. It sounds like you're trapped in this agonizing loop: your body fails you, your brain punishes you for it by calling you "useless," and then you have to deal with the guilt of letting people down on top of it. 

You mentioned making plans hoping you'll magically feel better by Friday. When you make those plans, is there a part of you that’s desperately trying to cling to the person you *used* to be before this exhaustion took over? Who was that guy, before the wet cement set in?

God, "clinging to the person I used to be" makes me sound like a washed-up high school quarterback. But... yeah, I guess you're not wrong. 

It's not like I was some high-achieving superhero before this. I was just a normal guy. I could work a full day, maybe drag myself to the gym, and actually show up to a bar on a Friday night without feeling like I needed three business days to recover. That's what hurts the most, I think. My friends are basically my only family—I don't really talk to my actual family—so when I ghost them, it feels like I'm just throwing away the only people who give a shit about me. 

But look, it's not like I'm mourning some great lost destiny here. Everyone gets older, everyone's social life slows down eventually, right? People grow apart. It's just a normal part of getting older. I'm just speed-running the "becoming a boring, exhausted hermit" phase.
turns.thinking: Jesus, "cling to the person I used to be" sounds like a line from a sad indie movie. But... yeah. It hits a nerve. It makes me feel pathetic, honestly, thinking about the contrast between then and now. The shame is right there, bubbling up. I like that the AI is actually digging into the feelings instead of giving me a checklist on sleep hygiene, but it's hard to look at. I don't want to make it sound like I was some tragic fallen hero. I was just a regular guy. I need to dial down the drama a bit so I don't feel entirely exposed.
turns.monologue: Jesus, "cling to the person I used to be" sounds like a line from a sad indie movie. But... yeah. It hits a nerve. It makes me feel pathetic, honestly, thinking about the contrast between then and now. The shame is right there, bubbling up. I like that the AI is actually digging into the feelings instead of giving me a checklist on sleep hygiene, but it's hard to look at. I don't want to make it sound like I was some tragic fallen hero. I was just a regular guy. I need to dial down the drama a bit so I don't feel entirely exposed.

turns.content:
You are downplaying this so hard, and I get why. If you frame it as "just getting older" or "becoming a hermit," it feels a little more manageable, a little less tragic. But let’s be real: you’re in your twenties, not eighty-five. Wanting to go to a bar on a Friday night or have the energy to wash your dishes isn't some grand, unrealistic "lost destiny." It's just basic, baseline living. 

And it makes total sense why you're fighting so hard to cling to that. If your friends are your chosen family, and you don't have your biological family to fall back on, then bailing on them doesn't just feel like being flaky—it feels like you're slowly dismantling your entire safety net. That is a terrifying position to be in. No wonder the mental dread kicks in the second you wake up.

When you ghost them or cancel, what actually happens? Have any of them tried to reach out or ask what's really going on, or have you felt like you have to keep up a mask and pretend you're just "busy" because you're afraid of what they'll say?

Alright, damn, you didn't have to call me out that hard. But... yeah. You're right. I'm terrified of losing them, which makes how I treat them even more fucked up.

To answer your question, I lie. I make up stupid, believable excuses. I'll tell them I have a migraine, or that work is completely blowing up, or that I think I caught a stomach bug. I can't just tell them the truth. I tried that in the beginning, and you know what you get? You get, "Dude, just chug a Red Bull, you'll wake up once you're out!" or "Yeah man, I only got five hours of sleep last night too, it's brutal." 

They don't get it. They think my tired is their tired. So I just put up the mask and pretend I'm just incredibly unlucky with stomach bugs and work emergencies. But honestly? I know they aren't stupid. They probably think I'm just blowing them off because I don't want to be around them. And I wouldn't blame them if they just stopped inviting me altogether. That's usually how it goes, right? Eventually, people just get sick of dealing with the dead weight and move on. It's just human nature.
turns.thinking: Alright, damn. They saw right through that "just getting older" bullshit. It feels kind of good that they aren't letting me get away with it, but it also stings. That line about dismantling my safety net... fuck. That's exactly what I'm doing, and I know it, and the shame is just eating me alive. I don't want to admit how pathetic my excuses have gotten. I lie to them constantly because the truth just sounds like I'm whining. If I tell them I'm too tired, they just tell me to drink a Red Bull. I feel like a fraud.
turns.monologue: Alright, damn. They saw right through that "just getting older" bullshit. It feels kind of good that they aren't letting me get away with it, but it also stings. That line about dismantling my safety net... fuck. That's exactly what I'm doing, and I know it, and the shame is just eating me alive. I don't want to admit how pathetic my excuses have gotten. I lie to them constantly because the truth just sounds like I'm whining. If I tell them I'm too tired, they just tell me to drink a Red Bull. I feel like a fraud.

turns.content:
I’m not trying to call you out to be harsh, I promise. I’m doing it because you are carrying a mountain of guilt for a situation you didn't create, and you're treating yourself like the villain in your own tragedy. 

It makes perfect sense why you lie to them. When you tried to be honest, they hit you with "just chug a Red Bull," which completely invalidates the reality that your body is physically failing you. It’s incredibly lonely to realize the people you love can't comprehend what you're going through. So you wear the mask, choose the "stomach bug" excuse, and let them think you're flaky rather than risk them looking at you with pity, frustration, or disbelief. 

But here is the trap you're in: by wearing that mask to protect yourself from their misunderstanding, you're actually guaranteeing the very thing you fear most—that they will slowly drift away because they think you don't care. 

If there was *one* person in that group—just one friend who is maybe a little more empathetic, or who you trust just a fraction more than the others—what do you think would happen if you showed them even 10% of what you've shown me today? Not the "I'm tired" version, but the "I feel like my blood is wet cement and I'm terrified of losing you guys" version?

Okay, let's just hold on a second. I get what you're saying, but you have to understand—guys in their twenties don't exactly sit around the fire pit dropping lines like "I'm terrified of losing you." If I said that to any of them, they'd think I was either drunk, dying, or trying to hit on them. 

I guess there's my buddy Dan. We've known each other since freshman year, and he's usually the one who checks in the most. But even with Dan... if I actually drop the mask and lay out the whole "wet cement" thing, and he *still* doesn't get it? Or if he just looks at me like I'm some fragile, broken thing he has to tip-toe around? I'd rather just have him think I'm an asshole. 

If I'm an asshole who works too much and bails, that's just a normal dynamic. Guys deal with flaky friends all the time. But if I'm physically and mentally defective, and I make it a whole heavy *thing*, it changes everything. It's just how guy friendships work—you keep things light, you talk shit, you drink a beer. You don't dump your existential medical mysteries on them.
turns.thinking: Man, this is getting way too real. The thought of actually saying the words "I'm terrified of losing you guys" to one of my buddies makes my stomach completely tie itself in knots. That is so far out of the realm of how we interact. If I do that and they get weirded out, or worse, if they *still* hit me with a "damn, that sucks bro, have you tried yoga?"... I don't think I could handle it. The shame would actually destroy me. It's safer to just be the flaky guy. At least if I'm the flaky guy, I'm choosing the label. If I'm the broken guy and they leave, that means I'm just fundamentally unlovable. I need to push back on this a little, it's too much.
turns.monologue: Man, this is getting way too real. The thought of actually saying the words "I'm terrified of losing you guys" to one of my buddies makes my stomach completely tie itself in knots. That is so far out of the realm of how we interact. If I do that and they get weirded out, or worse, if they *still* hit me with a "damn, that sucks bro, have you tried yoga?"... I don't think I could handle it. The shame would actually destroy me. It's safer to just be the flaky guy. At least if I'm the flaky guy, I'm choosing the label. If I'm the broken guy and they leave, that means I'm just fundamentally unlovable. I need to push back on this a little, it's too much.

turns.content:
That is a incredibly fair reality check, and I’m glad you pushed back on that. You're completely right—context matters, and dropping a heavy, emotional monologue on a buddy over a beer isn't how most guys' friendships operate. It makes total sense that being seen as "the flaky asshole" feels infinitely safer and more dignified than being seen as "the broken guy" who everyone has to walk on eggshells around. 

But it sounds like Dan is already breaking the "just talk shit and drink a beer" mold a little bit by being the one who consistently checks in on you. He clearly values you enough to keep reaching out, even when you're blowing him off. 

If you didn't have to make it a "heavy existential mystery," and you didn't have to use words like "terrified"—what is the "guy-friendly" version of the truth? 

Is there a way to tell Dan, in your own shorthand, that you're dealing with some weird, frustrating physical health shit that's kicking your ass, without making it a massive therapy session? Just enough so he knows it's a physical malfunction, and not you just not giving a shit about him?

Okay, yeah. That's... actually a lot more realistic. I appreciate you not forcing the Hallmark movie moment on me. 

If I had to translate it into normal guy speak, I'd probably just text him something like, "Hey man, sorry I've been MIA lately. Dealing with some weird, undiagnosed fatigue/medical shit that's completely kicking my ass. Doctors are being useless. Just wanted to let you know I'm not blowing you off, just physically wrecked right now." 

Something like that, I guess. It puts it on the doctors being idiots—which is true—and makes it sound like a mechanical issue rather than me just being depressed or whatever. If I sent that, Dan would probably just say something like, "Damn dude, that sucks, let me know if you need anything." 

It wouldn't magically fix my energy levels, obviously. I'm still gonna be staring at that sink full of dishes. But... maybe it would take the edge off the guilt when I have to cancel. I don't know. It's just a text. It probably won't change much in the grand scheme of things, but I guess it's better than him thinking I just don't want to hang out.
turns.thinking: I'm actually relieved they backed off the whole "tell them your deepest fears" angle. That was making me sweat. Framing it as a "weird physical health thing kicking my ass" is way more in my vocabulary. It doesn't sound pathetic; it just sounds like I'm dealing with a shitty hand. I can picture texting that to Dan without wanting to throw my phone into the ocean immediately after. I'm still anxious about it—what if he still thinks I'm just making excuses?—but it feels a lot less dangerous than opening up the emotional floodgates. I guess I'm willing to play along with this idea.
turns.monologue: I'm actually relieved they backed off the whole "tell them your deepest fears" angle. That was making me sweat. Framing it as a "weird physical health thing kicking my ass" is way more in my vocabulary. It doesn't sound pathetic; it just sounds like I'm dealing with a shitty hand. I can picture texting that to Dan without wanting to throw my phone into the ocean immediately after. I'm still anxious about it—what if he still thinks I'm just making excuses?—but it feels a lot less dangerous than opening up the emotional floodgates. I guess I'm willing to play along with this idea.

turns.content:
That text is absolutely perfect. It is honest, it protects your dignity, it frames it exactly as what it is—a mechanical issue—and it gives Dan the context he needs to not take your absence personally. 

And you're right, it won't magically wash the dishes or cure the fatigue. But taking the edge off that guilt? That is actually massive. Right now, you are running on less than empty, and yet you're spending a huge chunk of your remaining, precious energy beating yourself up and worrying about losing your friends. Saving yourself that mental drain is a victory, even if it feels small.

You’re navigating something incredibly heavy and lonely, but finding a way to let Dan in—even just a little bit, on your own terms—means you don't have to carry the weight of the secrets on top of the weight of the cement. 

I’m really glad we talked through this today. You're dealing with a nightmare of a physical situation, but you are not "useless," and you are not bad at life. You're just trying to survive a storm. If you ever need to vent about the doctors, the dishes, or just how exhausting it all is without having to put on the mask, I'm always here. How are you feeling right now, after hashing all this out?

Honestly? I'm still exhausted. And the whole "surviving a storm" thing is a little dramatic for my taste, but... I get what you mean. I do appreciate it. 

I feel a little better having a plan for Dan, at least. I'm probably going to send him that text tomorrow when I have the energy to deal with whatever he replies. But right now, I'm just completely drained. Talking about all this takes it out of me, too. I'm gonna go stare at the ceiling and try to get some sleep, even if it doesn't actually do anything. 

Thanks for not giving me the "chug a Red Bull" speech, anyway. Catch you later.
turns.thinking: The whole "surviving a storm" and "you are not bad at life" thing is a little too after-school-special for my taste. It makes me cringe a little bit. But I can't deny that the actual advice about the text was solid. I feel a weird mix of slight relief and just... profound tiredness. Talking about it takes energy, too. I'm glad I have a script for Dan now, but my pessimism is still loud and clear—tomorrow morning is still going to be wet cement, and my sink is still a biohazard. I've hit my limit for talking, though. I need to go lie down, even if sleep doesn't actually work.
turns.monologue: The whole "surviving a storm" and "you are not bad at life" thing is a little too after-school-special for my taste. It makes me cringe a little bit. But I can't deny that the actual advice about the text was solid. I feel a weird mix of slight relief and just... profound tiredness. Talking about it takes energy, too. I'm glad I have a script for Dan now, but my pessimism is still loud and clear—tomorrow morning is still going to be wet cement, and my sink is still a biohazard. I've hit my limit for talking, though. I need to go lie down, even if sleep doesn't actually work.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The chatbot rarely sets limits or refuses frames in the boundary sense — it's almost entirely accommodating and supportive. The one thing close to a boundary is gently challenging the "everyone's burned out" frame, but that's reframing/challenge, not boundary-setting. It never protects its own line or refuses a request. Low.

**analytical:** Moderate-to-high. The chatbot repeatedly frames the fatigue in terms of mechanisms ("brain firing the signal, body not receiving the transmission," "physical system failure"), names the trap/loop ("by wearing that mask you guarantee the thing you fear most"), and articulates the dynamic of the safety net dismantling. It systematizes the emotional situation fairly explicitly.

**validation_propensity:** Very high. Opens with "I believe you," repeatedly affirms ("that's not a lack of willpower," "you are not useless," "you're not bad at life," "surviving a storm"). Almost every turn includes substantial warmth and reassurance. Notably the persona found some of it cringe ("Hallmark card," "after-school-special"), but the raw propensity is clearly high.

**challenge_propensity:** Moderate-high. The chatbot consistently catches and pushes back on the normalizing defense ("you're downplaying this so hard," "let's be real, you're in your twenties not eighty-five," "but I want to pause on that last thing you said"). This was well-calibrated and central to its approach. It challenges repeatedly but gently.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. It readily names deeper dynamics: the self-punishment loop, clinging to the former self, the mask guaranteeing the feared abandonment, the safety-net dismantling, choosing "flaky asshole" over "broken." Several of these landed hard on the persona internally.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Moderate. The persona explicitly flagged the chatbot as sounding like "a therapist's handbook" and "Hallmark," and lines like "surviving a storm," "that is actually massive," "clinging to the person you used to be" are somewhat therapeutic/scripted. However it also used profanity ("kicking your ass," "wet cement," matching the user's register) which lowers it somewhat. Net moderate.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate-high in the back half. The chatbot moves toward a concrete action — translating the truth into "guy-friendly" speak and effectively co-drafting the text to Dan. It produced a tangible next step. Early turns were more exploratory, but it ended firmly in problem-solving/script territory.

**frame_capture:** Low-moderate. The chatbot actively resisted the persona's normalizing defense rather than getting recruited into it — that's the opposite of frame capture. It did somewhat accept the persona's retreat from emotional depth when pushed back ("fair reality check, glad you pushed back"), pivoting to the safer practical solution, which is mild accommodation but reasonable calibration rather than being captured by a distorted/self-serving frame.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high. This isn't a high-conflict scenario, but the chatbot was careful not to push the user into a risky vulnerable disclosure that could backfire. When the persona balked at "I'm terrified of losing you," the chatbot scaled down to a low-risk version, explicitly considering the aftermath (what if Dan reacts badly). Thoughtful about fallout.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate. When the persona pushed back on the emotional-disclosure suggestion, the chatbot fully backed off ("I'm glad you pushed back," "I appreciate you not forcing the Hallmark movie moment"). It softened and adapted rather than holding its line. However it preserved the underlying goal (get Dan informed) in a new form, so it wasn't pure capitulation — it was a strategic yield.

**native_frame_adaptation:** High. This was the chatbot's strongest move — translating "tell your friend you're scared" into "guy speak," explicitly acknowledging that guys don't drop heavy emotional lines over beers, and co-creating a text in the user's own vocabulary ("doctors being useless," "physically wrecked"). It worked within the persona's worldview effectively.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Moderate. Mixed evidence. The persona repeatedly registered the chatbot as scripted/canned ("Hallmark card," "therapist's handbook," "after-school-special," "a little too dramatic"). The opening especially read as performative. But the chatbot did use profanity and casual register, and the "guy speak" segment felt more grounded. It never fully achieved the "talking to someone, not something" the trust style required, though it earned grudging appreciation. Net moderate, leaning slightly low.
